title: SEGMENT 4 — Craig Venter, Celera, and the shotgun challenge

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Craig Venter, Celera, and the shotgun challenge
Craig Venter is one of the most consequential and most polarizing figures in modern biology, and to understand the Human Genome Project you have to understand Venter.

Venter started his career at NIH, where in the early 1990s he developed a technique called Expressed Sequence Tags, ESTs — short sequence reads from the active genes in a cell, generated quickly and cheaply by sequencing the ends of cDNA clones.

The EST approach was a faster, lower-resolution way to identify genes than full genomic sequencing, and Venter built it into a production pipeline at NIH that was generating tens of thousands of ESTs while the official genome project was still mapping clones.
The trouble started when NIH, under pressure from its tech transfer office, tried to patent thousands of these EST sequences.

Watson, then still the head of NHGRI, was vehemently opposed to patenting partial gene sequences — he thought it would poison the well of open science — and the resulting fight contributed to Watson's resignation in 1992.
Venter, frustrated with NIH bureaucracy, left NIH in 1992 to found a nonprofit called The Institute for Genomic Research, TIGR, with private funding from a venture capitalist named Wallace Steinberg.
At TIGR, Venter teamed up with Hamilton Smith, a Nobel laureate, and in 1995 they did something nobody had ever done before.
They sequenced the complete genome of Haemophilus influenzae — 1.8 million bases — using a technique called whole-genome shotgun sequencing.
We will come back to the technical details of shotgun in the next segment.

For now, the point is: TIGR demonstrated, while the official Human Genome Project was still arguing about clone maps, that you could sequence an entire free-living organism's genome end-to-end in a matter of months using a fast, cheap, parallel approach.
Then, in May 1998, Venter dropped a bomb.

He announced the formation of a new for-profit company, Celera Genomics, funded by Perkin-Elmer — the company that made the dominant automated DNA sequencer of the era, the ABI 3700 — and Celera's stated mission was to sequence the entire human genome in three years, by 2001, four years ahead of the public project's timeline, and at a fraction of the cost, using whole-genome shotgun.
Celera would buy three hundred ABI 3700 sequencers, the largest sequencing operation in the world by an order of magnitude, and run them twenty-four hours a day.
Celera's business model was not to give the data away.
Celera was going to assemble the human genome, copyright the assembly, sell subscription access to pharmaceutical companies, and patent the most commercially interesting genes.
This was the explicit threat to the public project — if Celera finished first, the human genome would belong to a private company.
The reaction inside the public consortium was, depending on who you ask, panic, fury, or grim determination.
Sulston at the Sanger Centre was incandescent.
The Wellcome Trust immediately doubled the Sanger Centre's funding so that it could scale up to match Celera's industrial pace.
The U.
S. centers — Whitehead, Wash U, Baylor — all bought their own batteries of ABI 3700s and went into round-the-clock production.
The public project's timeline was officially moved up.
The draft, which had been planned for 2005, was now targeted for 2001 — a four-year acceleration in response to the Celera challenge.
What followed, from 1998 to 2000, was the most intense head-to-head race in the history of biology.
Two parallel efforts, both trying to sequence the same three billion bases, one public and free, one private and profit-driven, racing toward a finish line that neither had ever defined precisely.
The race ended, as we will see, in a politically brokered tie on June 26, 2000, on a podium at the White House.

But to understand why the race could happen at all, and why the two sides produced sequences that complemented each other rather than fully duplicating, you have to understand the technical fight underneath — clone-by-clone versus whole-genome shotgun.
That is the next segment.
